retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Post-stroke depression, motor recovery, and the role of SSRI
retrieved_docs.content: FLAME (Fluoxetine on Motor Rehabilitation After Ischemic Stroke) trial [2011] showed improved motor recovery in patients with hemiplegia and hemiparesis with the use of fluoxetine. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Dipyridamole -- Indications -- Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, a 2005 meta-analysis performed by Leonardi-Bee et al compiled data from randomized controlled trials and suggested that dipyridamole alone reduced the risk of recurrent stroke events compared to control arms. [8] However, the exact impact and validity of this finding are debated, as 59% of the data collected from this meta-analysis was derived from the ESPS-2 trial; excluding ESPS-2, the results were no longer statistically significant.

retrieved_docs.title: Dipyridamole -- Indications -- Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: Stroke prevention: The indication for stroke prevention is primarily based on data obtained from the European Stroke Prevention Study 2 Trial (ESPS-2). [7] This randomized controlled trial evaluated stroke risk reduction in patients with recent transient ischemic attacks (TIA) or ischemic stroke. The patients meeting the inclusion criteria were categorized into 4 distinct treatment groups: placebo treatment, aspirin 25 mg twice daily, extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg twice daily, or a combination of aspirin 25 mg and extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg twice daily.

retrieved_docs.title: Dipyridamole -- Indications -- Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: A 2009 study published in the New England Journal of Medicine compared the combination of ASA-ERDP with clopidogrel for preventing the risk of recurrent stroke. [9] This double-blinded study evaluated patients receiving aspirin 25 mg with extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg twice daily versus clopidogrel 75 mg once daily. The study demonstrated comparable outcomes in the primary endpoint of stroke recurrence, with similar rates between groups: 8.8% in the clopidogrel group and 9.0% in the ASA-ERDP group. The results indicated that neither treatment was superior in preventing stroke recurrence.

retrieved_docs.title: Dipyridamole -- Indications -- Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: The ESPS-2 study concluded that the group that received the combination therapy with aspirin (25 mg) and extended-release dipyridamole (200 mg) showed a reduced risk of stroke compared to the placebo group. Furthermore, the combination treatment group demonstrated the most significant reduction in stroke risk, with an odds ratio (OR) of 0.59, compared to aspirin (OR=0.79) and extended-release dipyridamole (OR=0.81).

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Heparin after an Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: TOAST (Trial of ORG 10172 in Acute Stroke Treatment) trial [1998] showed no favorable 3-months outcomes with the use of heparinoids after an acute ischemic stroke. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Anticoagulation(Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The guidelines also recommend further clinical trials and suggest a lack of well-established data for the use of direct thrombin inhibitors and factor Xa inhibitors, including argatroban, dabigatran, etc., as monotherapy or adjunctive therapy to alteplase in AIS. [11] The studies demonstrating the safety and efficacy of using thrombin inhibitors or factor Xa inhibitors have small populations, limiting the applicability of the results. The safety and feasibility of dabigatran within 24 hours of minor strokes or transient ischemic attacks (TIA), defined as NIHSS score less than or equal to 3, was studied in N=53 patients. The study found that initiating dabigatran was feasible as no patients experienced a symptomatic hemorrhagic stroke; however, a larger randomized controlled trial is needed to confirm safety. [22] Another study looked at the safety of direct thrombin inhibitor, argatroban, in conjunction with tPA. The open-label, pilot study, known as ARTSS-2 (Argatroban with Recombinant Tissue Plasminogen Activator for Acute Stroke), included N=90 patients and found that rates of intracerebral hemorrhage were comparable among control and study arm groups; however, a larger trial is needed to determine efficacy. [23] Additionally, a post hoc analysis from a multicenter prospective study, CROMIS-2 (Clinical Relevance of Microbleeds in Stroke-2), reviewed initiation of anticoagulation timing post-ischemic strokes and looked at 90-day clinical outcomes. The results included N=1355 patients and found that initiation of oral anticoagulation (OAC) early-on (0 to 4 days) vs. later (greater than or equal to 5 days or never started) post-stroke associated with atrial fibrillation had similar odds for the composite endpoint of ischemic stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, and death at 90 days. [23] However, the data is still insufficient to change practice as adequately powered controlled randomized trials are still needed. [11] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Dextroamphetamine-Amphetamine -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Dextroamphetamine-amphetamine has been used historically in the treatment of cerebrovascular accidents. Research by Walker-Batson et al [4] and Crisostomo et al [ Annals of Neurology ] showed that amphetamines improved motor function in patients with ischemic stroke compared to those receiving physical therapy alone. However, a subsequent study reported conflicting results, with no significant difference in Fugl-Meyer motor scale scores between the amphetamine and placebo groups. Recent clinical studies investigating dextroamphetamine for poststroke recovery have shown no success.

retrieved_docs.title: Broca Aphasia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Medical treatment of aphasia is currently under investigation in clinical trials. Drug therapies have included catecholaminergic agents (bromocriptine, levodopa, amantadine, dexamphetamine), piracetam and related compounds, acetylcholine esterase inhibitors, and neurotrophic factors. Previous studies have been small, and further studies are needed to determine the efficacy of these pharmacological agents. Also, transcranial magnetic stimulation and transcranial direct stimulation trials for aphasia are currently underway. When the cause of Broca aphasia is a stroke, recovery of language function peaks within 2 to 6 months, after which time further progress is limited. However, patients should be encouraged to work on speech production because cases of improvement have been seen long after a stroke. There are commercial software products available that claim to improve language function, but for the most part, these have not been rigorously tested in randomized clinical trials. It is important to address issues of post-stroke depression and post-stroke cognitive impairment, as well as disorders of executive function, awareness, neglect, and hemiparesis during the rehabilitation process to optimize the outcome for an individual patient. Family and social support are extremely important to keep patients with language deficits engaged in social and leisure activities, which can greatly influence the aphasic patient’s quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Dieting Approaches to Treatment of Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- I) Drugs of historical significance/withdrawn from the market for the treatment of obesity
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid hormone, dinitrophenol, amphetamines, aminorex, phenylpropanolamine, and ephedra alkaloids were used in the past for weight loss but subsequently withdrawn by the FDA for this specific indication due to side effects of sympathetic overactivity. Fenfluramine and dexfenfluramine were banned due to their association with heart disease. Rimonabant, a cannabinoid receptor 1 antagonist/inverse agonist, was also banned as it was associated with depression and suicide. Sibutramine was associated with a high risk of cardiovascular disease and stroke. Lorcaserin was the latest drug to be withdrawn from the market by the FDA in February 2020 after the CAMELLIA-TIMI 61 trial showed that patients who were treated with lorcaserin long-term were at high risk of pancreatic, colorectal, and lung cancers. [9] [1] [7] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Dipyridamole -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the FDA-approved and off-label indications for dipyridamole use, including stroke prevention, mechanical valve adjunct therapy, and pharmacological stress testing. Implement appropriate monitoring protocols of dipyridamole during pharmacological stress testing and thromboembolism prophylaxis to ensure optimal patient care. Select the appropriate dosage forms and administration routes of dipyridamole based on clinical indications and patient characteristics. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare providers to optimize dipyridamole therapy, enhance patient safety, and minimize adverse events. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Stroke -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Based on a longitudinal observational study of the Swedish Stroke Register (Riksstroke), it was found that the survival rates differed between patients with ischemic stroke and those with ICH. At 30 days after a first stroke, 89.9% of patients with ischemic stroke were alive compared to 69.3% of patients with ICH. Although early mortality was higher for patients diagnosed with ICH, beyond 30 days, survival declined at a similar rate for both groups. However, the proportion of functionally dependent survivors remained consistently higher for patients with ICH at all time points. The study found that at 5 years, the survival rate was 49.4% for ischemic stroke and 37.8% for ICH. The authors concluded that 5 years after a first stroke, over 65% of patients with ischemic stroke and over 75% of patients with ICH were either deceased or functionally dependent. [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Issues of Concern -- The Drip-and-Ship Strategy
retrieved_docs.content: In their research, Sheth and colleagues examined data from a substantial cohort of 44,667 patients who experienced ischemic strokes and received intravenous tPA treatment within 3 hours of symptom onset. [28] These data were sourced from the Get With The Guidelines-Stroke program, encompassing the period from April 2003 to October 2010 and involving 1440 hospitals. The study findings revealed that approximately 1 in 4 AIS patients who received tPA were treated through the drip-and-ship strategy. The outcomes for patients following this approach exhibited modest differences compared to those who were directly admitted to a stroke center and received tPA. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Lacunar Stroke -- Treatment / Management -- Lacunar Stroke Management
retrieved_docs.content: Management of a patient who presents with acute lacunar infarct out of the tPA window consistent with a noncardioembolic stroke includes dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel within 24 hours of symptom onset and continued for 21 days. Dual antiplatelet therapy in the acute phase effectively lowers recurrent ischemic stroke for 90 days from symptom onset. This is supported by the Clopidogrel in High-Risk Patients with Acute Nondisabling Cerebrovascular Events (CHANCE) and the Platelet-Oriented Inhibition in New TIA and Minor Ischemic Stroke (POINT) trials. These studies were performed on patients with all types of ischemic stroke but supported for use in lacunar strokes in subgroup analysis. Antiplatelet therapy should be delayed for 24 hours in patients who received tPA. [34] Patients with stuttering lacunar infarcts may also benefit from intravenous tPA.

retrieved_docs.title: Ischemic Stroke -- Treatment / Management -- Blood pressure
retrieved_docs.content: Labetalol: Can be initiated at 10 to 20 mg IV; may repeat once if needed. Nicardipine: Can be started at 5 mg/h IV and increased by 2.5 mg/h every 5 to 15 minutes as needed. The maximum dose is 15 mg/h. Clevidipine: Can be initiated at 1 to 2 mg/h IV. The dosage can be doubled every 15 minutes, but should not exceed 21 mg/h. Hydralazine and enalaprilat: May be considered based on the clinical scenario.

retrieved_docs.title: Tizanidine -- Indications -- Clinical Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Studies exhibit that tizanidine, baclofen, and diazepam effectively decreased excessive muscle tone in patients with multiple sclerosis or cerebrovascular lesions. Muscle strength improved in all three treatment groups, but the improvement was most significant with tizanidine. Shakespeare et al. also reported similar findings showing no differences in efficacy between tizanidine, baclofen, and dantrolene compared to diazepam. However, diazepam was associated with more sedation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 1298
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم) is placed in the Physical & Psychosomatic Disorders subcluster and is associated with a wide range of autonomic nervous system disorders and systemic sclerosis. Clinically it frequently co‑occurs with genitourinary symptom clusters and ill‑defined conditions, and is commonly observed alongside procedures such as transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) performed for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد) and prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا). Hypertension also co‑exists with renal calculi (حصاة حالبية), nausea and vomiting, and partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) for thyroid gland (دراق) disease. Major cardiovascular complications linked to hypertension include chronic atrial fibrillation, carotid bruit, multiple intracerebral hemorrhage, and cerebrovascular accident (stroke) (حادثة وعائية دماغية), which is classified under circulatory diseases and central nervous system vascular disease and is closely related to cerebral arteriosclerosis (تصلب الشرايين المخية). Cerebral arteriosclerosis contributes to the development of multiple lacunar infarcts, representing small‑vessel cerebrovascular disease. Pharmacologic management of both hypertension and BPH utilizes α1‑adrenergic antagonists—doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesylate, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride, and terazosin anhydrous—which relieve blood pressure elevation and urinary obstruction. In contrast, testosterone propionate is contraindicated in BPH because it may exacerbate prostatic enlargement. These interrelated disease entities and therapeutic strategies illustrate the multifaceted nature of hypertension, its extensive associations with genitourinary, cerebrovascular, and endocrine conditions, and the integrated medical and surgical approaches employed in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 571
retrieved_docs.content: for BPH and prostatic enlargement and is also associated with the diagnosis and management of prostate cancer. Therapeutically, several α₁‑adrenergic antagonists—doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesilát, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride, and terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous—are identified as potential treatments for hypertension, underscoring their role in managing hypertension and its extensive comorbidities. The interrelationships among transient vascular events, chronic cerebrovascular pathology, and prostatic conditions highlight the need for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2302
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine with ischemic complication is a broader clinical category that encompasses forms of migraine in which vascular ischemia occurs, and persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is a specific manifestation within this category. Persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is also classified as an acute cerebrovascular disease, reflecting its nature as a sudden, stroke‑like event caused by interruption of cerebral blood flow during a prolonged migraine aura. By linking migraine with ischemic complication to acute cerebrovascular disease through the specific entity of persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, the knowledge underscores that certain prolonged migraine auras can evolve into acute cerebral infarctions, highlighting the importance of recognizing these migraine‑related ischemic complications for timely diagnosis and appropriate stroke‑focused management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 680
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate and kabergolin are dopaminergic agents indicated for Parkinson’s disease but are contraindicated in patients with hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم). Chloromethiazol may also be employed in Parkinson’s disease. α‑Blockers such as doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesilát, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride and terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous are used to manage hypertension. Hypertension is clinically associated with autonomic nervous system disorders (اضطرابات الجهاز العصبي المستقل), nausea and vomiting (أعراض الغثيان والقيء), cardiomyopathy (اعتلال عضلة القلب), abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية), palpitations (خفقانات), chronic atrial fibrillation (رجفان أذيني مزمن) and benign prostatic hyperplasia (فرط تنسج بروستاتي حميد). Patients undergoing transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) often have underlying hypertension, and TURP is performed for benign prostatic hyperplasia or prostatic enlargement (تضخم بروستاتي). Parkinson’s disease is characteristically associated with tremor (رعاش) and a range of neuropsychiatric manifestations, including depressive disorders (اضطرابات اكتئابية), abnormal mental states, depression, anxiety and psychosis, underscoring the importance of monitoring mood symptoms. These interrelationships highlight the need for careful selection of dopaminergic therapy in hypertensive patients, the role of α‑blockers for blood‑pressure control, and the recognition of multiple cardiovascular, urological, neurological and psychiatric comorbidities in comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is described as having a transitory clinical course, indicating that its neurological deficits are brief and resolve quickly. This condition corresponds to the Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, which denotes the same short‑lasting, reversible episode of cerebral ischemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2126
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2354
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria basilaris is identified as the anatomical location where transient cerebral ischemia occurs. Transient cerebral ischemia, also referred to by its Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, represents a brief episode of reduced cerebral blood flow that can produce temporary neurological deficits. Recognizing that transient cerebral ischemia originates in the basilar artery is important for clinicians, as ischemic events in this posterior circulation can lead to distinct clinical presentations and influence diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesilát, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride, and terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous are all alpha‑1 adrenergic antagonists that may be used to treat فرط ضغط الدم (hypertension). Hypertension is clinically associated with a range of manifestations, including أعراض الغثيان والقيء (nausea and vomiting), اعتلال عضلة القلب (cardiomyopathy), خفقانات (palpitations), and رجفان أذيني مزمن (chronic atrial fibrillation). In addition, hypertension has a broader relationship with اضطرابات الجهاز العصبي المستقل (autonomic nervous system disorders), reflecting the impact of elevated blood pressure on autonomic regulation. Recognizing these therapeutic options and associated clinical features is essential for comprehensive management of patients with hypertension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2493
retrieved_docs.content: Mefobarbital may be used to treat generalized onset seizures, offering therapeutic benefit in controlling these seizure episodes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2452
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia, referred to in Arabic as نوبة إقفارية عابرة, denotes a brief, reversible episode of reduced blood flow to a focal area of the brain that does not result in permanent tissue damage. This condition is characterized by temporary neurological deficits that resolve within minutes to hours and serves as an important clinical warning sign for the potential development of a full‑blown stroke. Recognizing the relationship between transient cerebral ischemia and نوبة إقفارية عابرة is essential for prompt evaluation, risk stratification, and implementation of preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of subsequent cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: audible thigh bruits, and diabetes mellitus without complications. Alpha‑blockers (doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesylate, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride, anhydrous terazosin) and other α‑1 adrenergic antagonists are therapeutic options for hypertension and for hyperplasia of the prostate, while amiloride hydrochloride is indicated for cystic fibrosis. Surgical management of prostatic enlargement and prostate cancer frequently involves transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP); prostate hyperplasia is contraindicated with testosterone propionate. Within the nervous system, circulatory diseases and syndromes of the nervous system encompass cerebrovascular accidents under transient cerebral ischemia, and cerebral artery sclerosis is directly related to stroke. Impaired social communication disorder is recognized both as a neuropsychiatric condition and as an indicator of chronic renal failure, illustrating the overlap between neuro‑psychosomatic and renal assessment. The

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2744
retrieved_docs.content: Acute cerebrovascular disease is classified as a subtype within the broader condition of persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, indicating that episodes of acute cerebrovascular disease are considered manifestations of the persistent migraine aura syndrome when it is accompanied by cerebral infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: (acute disorder of hemodialysis, first‑use syndrome, dialysis disequilibrium syndrome) as well as the syndrome of dialysis dysfunction. Hypertension (child condition of the kidneys) co‑occurs with genitourinary symptoms, autonomic nervous system disturbances, nausea, vomiting, renal dysfunction, and cerebrovascular complications such as hypertensive brain disease, acute cerebrovascular accidents, cerebral arterial sclerosis, multiple lacunar infarcts, transient cerebral ischemia (transient ischemic attacks) and fainting (غشي). Additional systemic associations include systemic sclerosis, tobacco use, multifocal atherosclerotic stenosis, chronic atrial fibrillation, gait impairment, syncope, benign prostatic hyperplasia, prostate cancer, low transverse cesarean section, chronic kidney disease, audible thigh bruits, and diabetes mellitus without complications. Alpha‑blockers (doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesylate, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride, anhydrous terazosin) and other

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2673
retrieved_docs.content: The basilar artery (arteria basilaris) is identified as a common site where transient cerebral ischemia can occur. Transient cerebral ischemia typically follows a transitory clinical course, indicating that its symptoms are brief and reversible. In Arabic, transient cerebral ischemia is referred to as “نوبة إقفارية عابرة,” underscoring its nature as a short‑lasting ischemic episode.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: Late effects of cerebrovascular disease has parent اضطراب مخي وعائي, indicating that the long‑term sequelae of cerebrovascular disease are classified under the broader category of cerebrovascular disorder. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians organize diagnoses, anticipate chronic complications, and tailor long‑term management strategies for patients who have experienced cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 231
retrieved_docs.content: surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque—endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان)—is indicated in the context of arterial thrombosis or embolism. The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.
